ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised chicken heart as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for specific cooked organ meats is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised chicken heart is approximately 80g, equivalent to 4–6 whole chicken hearts as served in a hawker or home-cooked meal context.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Heart, Braised — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (Food Code F11010016)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database, food code F11010016, Braised Chicken Heart. All core macros (energy 185 kcal, protein 26.41g, fat 7.92g, carbohydrate 0.1g) and reported micronutrients (sodium 48mg, potassium 132mg, calcium 19mg, iron 9.03mg, cholesterol 242mg, dietary fibre 0.0g) reflect the HPB canonical record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (2.3g/100g) adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken heart (NDB 05028) as HPB does not report saturated fat fractions separately for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for braised chicken heart as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.1g/100g). Per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology and peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008), GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of at least 10g per serving; high-protein, near-zero-carbohydrate organ meats such as chicken heart do not meet this threshold. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. No GI value is assigned.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (48mg/100g); high cholesterol warrants monitoring in combined dyslipidaemia
hypertension.guidance: Braised chicken heart is low in sodium at 48mg/100g and is generally suitable for hypertensive individuals; however, braising sauces containing soy sauce may add significant sodium, so patients should request minimal sauce or choose low-sodium preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.1g/100g); high protein supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.1g carbohydrate per 100g and 26.4g protein, braised chicken heart has a negligible glycaemic impact and is suitable as a protein source within a balanced Type 2 Diabetes meal plan, provided the braising sauce does not contain significant added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (242mg/100g); moderate saturated fat (2.3g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised chicken heart contains 242mg cholesterol per 100g, which is high; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, and current clinical guidelines recommend moderation of high-cholesterol organ meats for high-risk CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients; high cholesterol
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken heart is a poultry organ meat lacking meaningful omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or dietary fibre; it does not provide anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis patients and should be paired with omega-3-rich or antioxidant-rich foods, with moderation advised due to high cholesterol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised chicken heart (卤鸡心) is a cooked poultry organ meat dish prepared by gently braising whole chicken hearts in a seasoned liquid — typically soy sauce, aromatics, and spices — until tender and fully cooked through. The result is a firm, dense, and slightly chewy texture with a rich, savoury flavour profile characteristic of Chinese-style braised organ meats. It is commonly found in Singapore hawker centres, Chinese zi char stalls, and home kitchens as a side dish or accompaniment to rice. Nutritionally, braised chicken heart is a high-protein food (26.4g/100g) with moderate fat (7.92g/100g) and negligible carbohydrate (0.1g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. It is notably high in iron (9.03mg/100g), which is beneficial for individuals at risk of iron-deficiency anaemia, and contains significant dietary cholesterol (242mg/100g), which warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. Sodium content is low at 48mg/100g from the meat itself, though braising sauces may contribute additional sodium. The dish is halal-certified when prepared with appropriate ingredients and is a good source of complete protein for muscle maintenance and glycaemic stability in diabetic meal planning.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (F11010016) at confidence 0.95. The food identity is exact, the source is a primary approved regional database, and all core macros are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05028) as a secondary approved source.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.3g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken heart (NDB 05028), as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; this is documented as an adapted value. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific braised preparation are not reported in the HPB Nutrient Composition Database entry F11010016, and the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken heart (NDB 05028) does not provide all these values in a directly mappable form for this braised variant. Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; chicken heart contains no intrinsic sugar). Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (naturally zero for organ meat with no plant-based components). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.1g/100g); per University of Sydney GI Database methodology and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008), GI measurement requires a minimum available carbohydrate load and is not meaningful for high-protein near-zero-carbohydrate organ meats. FoodEx2 code A0CKN assigned as closest defensible match for cooked chicken heart; FoodOn URI FOODON_03301521 assigned for chicken heart food product. SNOMED CT code not available for this specific food product.
